6 Chapter 8: Markov Random Fields and Spatial Stochastic Processes

Markov random fields (MRFs) provide the mathematical foundation for modeling spatial dependencies in stochastic systems. Unlike the temporal Markov chains studied in Chapters 1 and 2, MRFs capture the conditional independence structure inherent in spatial data, where the distribution of a random variable at any location depends only on its neighbors. This chapter develops the theory of MRFs and their connection to spatial stochastic processes, with particular emphasis on Gibbs distributions, Gaussian MRFs, and their computational implementation.

6.1 8.1 Markov Random Fields: Definitions and Basic Properties

6.1.1 8.1.1 Graph-Theoretic Foundations

A Markov random field is defined with respect to an undirected graph that encodes the neighborhood structure of spatial dependencies.

Definition 8.1 (Graph and Neighborhood Structure). Let G = (V, E) be an undirected graph with vertex set V and edge set E. For any vertex i ∈ V, the neighborhood of i is N(i) = {j ∈ V : (i,j) ∈ E}. A subset C ⊆ V is called a clique if every pair of distinct vertices in C is connected by an edge.

The graph structure determines which random variables are conditionally independent given others. In spatial applications, vertices typically represent spatial locations (e.g., pixels in an image or regions in a map), and edges connect neighboring locations.

Definition 8.2 (Markov Random Field). Let X = (X_i : i ∈ V) be a collection of random variables indexed by vertices of graph G = (V, E). The process X is a Markov random field with respect to G if:

  1. P(X_i = x_i | X_j = x_j, j ≠ i) > 0 for all configurations x
  2. P(X_i = x_i | X_j = x_j, j ≠ i) = P(X_i = x_i | X_j = x_j, j ∈ N(i))

The second condition is the Markov property: each variable depends only on its graph neighbors.

6.1.2 8.1.2 The Hammersley-Clifford Theorem

The fundamental connection between MRFs and Gibbs distributions is established by the Hammersley-Clifford theorem, which provides both theoretical foundation and computational tractability.

Definition 8.3 (Gibbs Distribution). A probability distribution on X is called a Gibbs distribution with respect to graph G if it has the form:

P(X = x) = (1/Z) exp(-H(x))

where H(x) = Σ_{C ∈ C} ψ_C(x_C) is the energy function, C is the set of all cliques in G, ψ_C is a potential function on clique C, and Z = Σ_x exp(-H(x)) is the normalizing constant (partition function).

Theorem 8.1 (Hammersley-Clifford Theorem). Let X be a random field on graph G with positive probability for all configurations. Then X is a Markov random field with respect to G if and only if its distribution is a Gibbs distribution with respect to G.

Proof. We prove both directions.

(⟹) Assume X is an MRF. Define the energy function H(x) = -log P(x) + log Z. For any configuration x and vertex i, the conditional probability is:

P(X_i = x_i | X_j = x_j, j ≠ i) = P(x) / Σ_{x’_i} P(x’i, x{-i})

By the Markov property, this depends only on neighbors N(i). This implies that H(x) can be written as a sum over cliques containing i, establishing the Gibbs form.

(⟸) Assume P(x) is Gibbs. For any vertex i and configuration x, the conditional probability is:

P(X_i = x_i | X_{-i} = x_{-i}) = exp(-Σ_{C: i ∈ C} ψ_C(x_C)) / Σ_{x’i} exp(-Σ{C: i ∈ C} ψ_C(x’i, x{C{i}}))

Since cliques containing i involve only i and its neighbors, the conditional distribution depends only on X_j for j ∈ N(i), establishing the Markov property. □

This theorem is fundamental because it allows us to specify MRFs through energy functions, which often have natural interpretations in applications.

6.2 8.2 Classical Models: Ising and Potts Models

6.2.1 8.2.1 The Ising Model

The Ising model, originally developed in statistical physics, provides the canonical example of an MRF with binary variables.

Definition 8.4 (Ising Model). The Ising model on graph G with parameters β ∈ R and h ∈ R^V is the Gibbs distribution:

P(X = x) = (1/Z) exp(β Σ_{(i,j) ∈ E} x_i x_j + Σ_{i ∈ V} h_i x_i)

where x_i ∈ {-1, +1}, β is the interaction parameter, and h_i is the external field at vertex i.

The energy function H(x) = -β Σ_{(i,j) ∈ E} x_i x_j - Σ_{i ∈ V} h_i x_i encourages neighboring vertices to have the same sign when β > 0 (ferromagnetic interaction) or opposite signs when β < 0 (antiferromagnetic interaction).

Example 8.1: Two-Dimensional Ising Model on a Grid

Consider the Ising model on a 10×10 grid with periodic boundary conditions, β = 0.5, and h_i = 0. We implement Gibbs sampling to generate samples from this distribution.

using Random, StatsPlots, LinearAlgebra

struct IsingModel
    n::Int
    β::Float64
    h::Vector{Float64}
end

function neighbors(i::Int, j::Int, n::Int)
    # Return neighbors with periodic boundary conditions
    return [
        (mod1(i-1, n), j),
        (mod1(i+1, n), j), 
        (i, mod1(j-1, n)),
        (i, mod1(j+1, n))
    ]
end

function gibbs_step!(x::Matrix{Int}, model::IsingModel)
    n = model.n
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        # Compute sum of neighboring spins
        neighbor_sum = sum(x[ni, nj] for (ni, nj) in neighbors(i, j, n))
        
        # Compute conditional probabilities
        energy_diff = 2 * model.β * neighbor_sum + 2 * model.h[(i-1)*n + j]
        prob_plus = 1 / (1 + exp(-energy_diff))
        
        # Update spin
        x[i, j] = rand() < prob_plus ? 1 : -1
    end
end

function sample_ising(model::IsingModel, nsamples::Int, burnin::Int=1000)
    n = model.n
    x = rand([-1, 1], n, n)  # Random initialization
    
    # Burn-in
    for _ in 1:burnin
        gibbs_step!(x, model)
    end
    
    # Collect samples
    samples = Array{Int}(undef, n, n, nsamples)
    for s in 1:nsamples
        gibbs_step!(x, model)
        samples[:, :, s] = copy(x)
    end
    
    return samples
end

# Generate samples
model = IsingModel(10, 0.5, zeros(100))
samples = sample_ising(model, 100)

# Visualize final configuration
heatmap(samples[:, :, end], color=:RdBu, title="Ising Model Configuration")

The Gibbs sampler updates each spin according to its conditional distribution given all other spins. The periodic boundary conditions eliminate edge effects, and the positive β parameter encourages clustering of like spins.

6.2.2 8.2.2 The Potts Model

The Potts model generalizes the Ising model to more than two states per vertex.

Definition 8.5 (q-State Potts Model). The q-state Potts model on graph G is the Gibbs distribution:

P(X = x) = (1/Z) exp(β Σ_{(i,j) ∈ E} δ(x_i, x_j) + Σ_{i ∈ V} Σ_{k=1}^q h_{i,k} δ(x_i, k))

where x_i ∈ {1, 2, …, q}, δ is the Kronecker delta, β ∈ R, and h_{i,k} are external field parameters.

When q = 2 and we identify states {1,2} with {-1,+1}, the Potts model reduces to the Ising model. The Potts model exhibits rich phase behavior and is widely used in image segmentation and clustering applications.

struct PottsModel
    n::Int
    q::Int  # Number of states
    β::Float64
    h::Matrix{Float64}  # External fields (n×n × q)
end

function gibbs_step_potts!(x::Matrix{Int}, model::PottsModel)
    n, q = model.n, model.q
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        # Compute probabilities for each state
        probs = zeros(q)
        for k in 1:q
            # Energy from neighbors
            neighbor_energy = 0.0
            for (ni, nj) in neighbors(i, j, n)
                neighbor_energy += (x[ni, nj] == k) ? model.β : 0.0
            end
            
            # External field energy
            field_energy = model.h[i, j] # Simplified: assume uniform external field
            
            probs[k] = exp(neighbor_energy + field_energy)
        end
        
        # Normalize and sample
        probs ./= sum(probs)
        x[i, j] = sample(1:q, Weights(probs))
    end
end

6.3 8.3 Gaussian Markov Random Fields

Gaussian Markov random fields (GMRFs) provide a tractable class of MRFs with continuous variables, characterized by sparse precision matrices that encode the conditional independence structure.

6.3.1 8.3.1 Definition and Basic Properties

Definition 8.6 (Gaussian Markov Random Field). A random vector X ∈ R^n is a Gaussian Markov random field with respect to graph G = (V, E) if:

  1. X ~ N(μ, Σ) for some mean vector μ and covariance matrix Σ
  2. Q_{ij} = 0 ⟺ (i,j) ∉ E, where Q = Σ^{-1} is the precision matrix

The key insight is that zeros in the precision matrix correspond exactly to conditional independence relationships specified by the graph structure.

Theorem 8.2 (GMRF Characterization). Let X ~ N(μ, Q^{-1}) where Q is positive definite. Then X_i ⊥ X_j | X_{-{i,j}} if and only if Q_{ij} = 0.

Proof. The conditional distribution of (X_i, X_j) given X_{-{i,j}} is determined by the 2×2 submatrix of Q corresponding to indices i and j. Specifically, X_i ⊥ X_j | X_{-{i,j}} if and only if the off-diagonal elements of this conditional precision matrix are zero, which occurs if and only if Q_{ij} = 0. □

This theorem establishes the fundamental connection between graph structure and the sparsity pattern of the precision matrix.

6.3.2 8.3.2 Precision Matrix Structure and Computational Advantages

The sparse structure of GMRF precision matrices leads to significant computational advantages for inference and simulation.

Example 8.2: First-Order Autoregressive Structure on a Grid

Consider a GMRF on an n×n grid where each variable depends on its four nearest neighbors with equal correlation ρ.

using SparseArrays, Distributions, LinearAlgebra

function create_grid_precision(n::Int, κ::Float64, ρ::Float64)
    # Create precision matrix for grid GMRF
    # κ: marginal precision, ρ: spatial correlation
    N = n * n
    I_vec = Int[]
    J_vec = Int[]
    V_vec = Float64[]
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        idx = (i-1) * n + j
        
        # Diagonal element
        push!(I_vec, idx)
        push!(J_vec, idx)
        
        # Count neighbors
        num_neighbors = 0
        
        # Add neighbor connections
        for (di, dj) in [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]
            ni, nj = i + di, j + dj
            if 1 ≤ ni ≤ n && 1 ≤ nj ≤ n
                nidx = (ni-1) * n + nj
                push!(I_vec, idx)
                push!(J_vec, nidx)
                push!(V_vec, -κ * ρ)
                num_neighbors += 1
            end
        end
        
        # Diagonal value ensures proper normalization
        push!(V_vec, κ * (1 + (4 - num_neighbors) * ρ^2 / (1 - ρ^2)))
    end
    
    return sparse(I_vec, J_vec, V_vec, N, N)
end

function sample_gmrf(Q::SparseMatrixCSC, μ::Vector, nsamples::Int)
    # Efficient sampling using Cholesky factorization
    n = size(Q, 1)
    L = cholesky(Q).L
    samples = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n, nsamples)
    
    for i in 1:nsamples
        z = randn(n)
        # Solve Ly = z, then x = μ + L^T \ y
        y = L \ z
        samples[:, i] = μ + L' \ y
    end
    
    return samples
end

# Example usage
n = 20
κ = 1.0
ρ = 0.7
Q = create_grid_precision(n, κ, ρ)
μ = zeros(n^2)

samples = sample_gmrf(Q, μ, 100)

# Visualize a sample
sample_matrix = reshape(samples[:, 1], n, n)
heatmap(sample_matrix, title="GMRF Sample on 20×20 Grid")

The sparse structure of Q allows for efficient computation using sparse Cholesky factorization, reducing computational complexity from O(n^6) to O(n^3) for an n×n grid.

6.3.3 8.3.3 Intrinsic Gaussian Markov Random Fields

Many spatial applications require models that are invariant to global shifts, leading to intrinsic GMRFs with singular precision matrices.

Definition 8.7 (Intrinsic GMRF). An intrinsic Gaussian Markov random field is a GMRF where the precision matrix Q is positive semi-definite but singular, typically with rank(Q) = n - 1 for an n-dimensional random vector.

The canonical example is the intrinsic conditional autoregressive (ICAR) model used in spatial epidemiology:

function create_icar_precision(adjacency::SparseMatrixCSC)
    n = size(adjacency, 1)
    D = Diagonal(vec(sum(adjacency, dims=2)))  # Degree matrix
    return D - adjacency  # Graph Laplacian
end

# Example: Create adjacency matrix for a grid
function grid_adjacency(n::Int)
    N = n * n
    I_vec = Int[]
    J_vec = Int[]
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        idx = (i-1) * n + j
        for (di, dj) in [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]
            ni, nj = i + di, j + dj
            if 1 ≤ ni ≤ n && 1 ≤ nj ≤ n
                nidx = (ni-1) * n + nj
                push!(I_vec, idx)
                push!(J_vec, nidx)
            end
        end
    end
    
    return sparse(I_vec, J_vec, ones(length(I_vec)), N, N)
end

adj = grid_adjacency(10)
Q_icar = create_icar_precision(adj)
println("Rank of ICAR precision matrix: ", rank(Matrix(Q_icar)))

6.4 8.4 Connection Between Gaussian Processes and GMRFs

The relationship between Gaussian processes (GPs) and GMRFs provides a bridge between function-space and finite-dimensional perspectives on spatial modeling.

6.4.1 8.4.1 The SPDE Approach

The stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) approach, developed by Lindgren et al. (2011), establishes explicit connections between Matérn GPs and GMRFs through finite element methods.

Theorem 8.3 (Whittle-Matérn SPDE Representation). The Gaussian process with Matérn covariance function satisfies the SPDE:

(κ² - Δ)^{α/2} X(s) = W(s)

where κ > 0 is a scale parameter, α = ν + d/2 with ν > 0 the smoothness parameter and d the spatial dimension, Δ is the Laplacian operator, and W(s) is Gaussian white noise.

This representation allows us to approximate the GP by discretizing the SPDE using finite element methods, resulting in a GMRF whose precision matrix is determined by the finite element discretization.

using Ferrite, SparseArrays

function create_fem_matrices(grid_size::Int)
    # Create finite element matrices for SPDE discretization
    # This is a simplified implementation - full FEM requires more sophisticated mesh handling
    
    n = grid_size
    h = 1.0 / (n - 1)  # Grid spacing
    N = n * n
    
    # Mass matrix M and stiffness matrix K for finite differences
    I_vec, J_vec, M_vals, K_vals = Int[], Int[], Float64[], Float64[]
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        idx = (i-1) * n + j
        
        # Mass matrix (identity for finite differences)
        push!(I_vec, idx)
        push!(J_vec, idx)
        push!(M_vals, h^2)
        push!(K_vals, 4/h^2)  # Central difference Laplacian
        
        # Stiffness matrix (discrete Laplacian)
        for (di, dj) in [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]
            ni, nj = i + di, j + dj
            if 1 ≤ ni ≤ n && 1 ≤ nj ≤ n
                nidx = (ni-1) * n + nj
                push!(I_vec, idx)
                push!(J_vec, nidx)
                push!(M_vals, 0.0)
                push!(K_vals, -1/h^2)
            end
        end
    end
    
    M = sparse(I_vec, J_vec, M_vals, N, N)
    K = sparse(I_vec, J_vec, K_vals, N, N)
    
    return M, K
end

function spde_precision(κ::Float64, α::Float64, M::SparseMatrixCSC, K::SparseMatrixCSC)
    # Precision matrix for (κ²I - Δ)^α
    return (κ^2 * M + K)^α
end

# Example: Create SPDE-based GMRF approximation to Matérn GP
n = 50
M, K = create_fem_matrices(n)
κ = 2.0
α = 1.0  # Corresponds to ν = α - d/2 = 0.5 for d = 1

Q_spde = spde_precision(κ, α, M, K)
μ = zeros(n^2)

# Sample and visualize
samples_spde = sample_gmrf(Q_spde, μ, 1)
sample_field = reshape(samples_spde[:, 1], n, n)
heatmap(sample_field, title="SPDE-GMRF Approximation to Matérn GP")

6.4.2 8.4.2 Computational Advantages of the SPDE Approach

The SPDE representation provides several computational advantages over traditional GP methods:

  1. Sparse Linear Algebra: The precision matrix is sparse, enabling efficient factorization
  2. Scalability: Computational complexity scales as O(n^{3/2}) for n observations rather than O(n^3)
  3. Non-stationary Extensions: The SPDE framework naturally accommodates spatially-varying parameters

6.5 8.5 Simulation Methods for MRFs

Simulation from MRFs requires specialized algorithms due to the complex dependency structure and often intractable normalizing constants.

6.5.1 8.5.1 Gibbs Sampling

The Gibbs sampler iteratively updates each variable from its conditional distribution given all others.

function gibbs_sampler_mrf(initial_state, conditional_update!, niter::Int, burnin::Int=1000)
    state = deepcopy(initial_state)
    n_vars = length(state)
    
    # Storage for samples
    samples = Vector{typeof(state)}()
    
    # Burn-in
    for iter in 1:burnin
        for i in 1:n_vars
            conditional_update!(state, i)
        end
    end
    
    # Sampling
    for iter in 1:niter
        for i in 1:n_vars
            conditional_update!(state, i)
        end
        push!(samples, deepcopy(state))
    end
    
    return samples
end

# Example: Gibbs sampling for multivariate normal (GMRF)
function gmrf_conditional_update!(x::Vector, i::Int, Q::SparseMatrixCSC, μ::Vector)
    n = length(x)
    
    # Conditional mean: μᵢ - (1/Qᵢᵢ) Σⱼ≠ᵢ Qᵢⱼ(xⱼ - μⱼ)
    conditional_mean = μ[i]
    for j in 1:n
        if i != j && Q[i,j] != 0
            conditional_mean -= Q[i,j] * (x[j] - μ[j]) / Q[i,i]
        end
    end
    
    # Conditional variance: 1/Qᵢᵢ
    conditional_var = 1.0 / Q[i,i]
    
    # Sample from conditional distribution
    x[i] = conditional_mean + sqrt(conditional_var) * randn()
end

6.5.2 8.5.2 Perfect Simulation

Perfect simulation algorithms, such as coupling from the past, can generate exact samples from MRFs under certain conditions.

Theorem 8.4 (Coupling from the Past). For a monotonic MRF update function on a finite state space with a unique stationary distribution, coupling from the past produces exact samples in finite expected time.

The implementation requires careful handling of the coupling condition and monotonicity properties of the update function.

6.6 8.6 Applications in Spatial Statistics

6.6.1 8.6.1 Disease Mapping: The BYM Model

The Besag-York-Mollié (BYM) model combines spatially structured and unstructured random effects for modeling disease incidence across geographical regions.

Definition 8.8 (BYM Model). The BYM model for disease counts Y_i in region i is:

Y_i ~ Poisson(E_i θ_i) log(θ_i) = α + u_i + v_i

where E_i is the expected count, u_i ~ ICAR(τ_u) provides spatial structure, v_i ~ N(0, 1/τ_v) provides unstructured heterogeneity, and α is the intercept.

using Turing, StatsPlots

@model function bym_model(Y::Vector{Int}, E::Vector{Float64}, adj::SparseMatrixCSC)
    n = length(Y)
    
    # Priors
    α ~ Normal(0, 1)
    τ_u ~ Gamma(1, 1)
    τ_v ~ Gamma(1, 1)
    
    # Structured spatial effects (ICAR)
    u_raw ~ MvNormal(n, 1.0)
    # Transform to satisfy sum-to-zero constraint
    u = u_raw .- mean(u_raw)
    
    # Unstructured effects
    v ~ MvNormal(n, 1/τ_v)
    
    # Linear predictor
    η = α .+ sqrt(1/τ_u) .* u .+ v
    θ = exp.(η)
    
    # Likelihood
    for i in 1:n
        Y[i] ~ Poisson(E[i] * θ[i])
    end
end

# Example usage (synthetic data)
function generate_synthetic_disease_data(n::Int, adj::SparseMatrixCSC)
    E = ones(n)  # Uniform expected counts
    α_true = 0.1
    τ_u_true = 1.0
    τ_v_true = 2.0
    
    # Generate structured effects
    Q_icar = create_icar_precision(adj)
    # Add small diagonal for numerical stability
    Q_stable = Q_icar + 1e-6 * I
    u_true = rand(MvNormal(zeros(n), inv(Matrix(τ_u_true * Q_stable))))
    u_true .- mean(u_true)  # Center
    
    # Generate unstructured effects
    v_true = rand(MvNormal(zeros(n), I/τ_v_true))
    
    # Generate observations
    θ_true = exp.(α_true .+ u_true .+ v_true)
    Y = [rand(Poisson(E[i] * θ_true[i])) for i in 1:n]
    
    return Y, E, θ_true
end

# Generate data and fit model
n = 25  # 5×5 grid
adj = grid_adjacency(5)
Y, E, θ_true = generate_synthetic_disease_data(n, adj)

model = bym_model(Y, E, adj)
chain = sample(model, NUTS(), 1000)

# Extract posterior summaries
θ_posterior = group(chain, :θ)
plot(θ_true, label="True θ", xlabel="Region", ylabel="Relative Risk")
plot!(mean(θ_posterior.value, dims=1)[1,:], label="Posterior Mean θ")

6.6.2 8.6.2 Image Analysis and Segmentation

MRFs provide powerful tools for image analysis, particularly in segmentation tasks where spatial coherence is important.

Example 8.3: Image Segmentation with Potts Model

function image_segmentation_potts(image::Matrix{Float64}, k::Int, β::Float64, max_iter::Int=100)
    n, m = size(image)
    
    # Initialize random segmentation
    segmentation = rand(1:k, n, m)
    
    # Compute cluster centers
    centers = zeros(k)
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # Update cluster centers
        for c in 1:k
            mask = segmentation .== c
            centers[c] = sum(mask) > 0 ? mean(image[mask]) : mean(image)
        end
        
        # Update segmentation using ICM (Iterated Conditional Modes)
        changed = false
        for i in 1:n, j in 1:m
            # Compute energy for each label
            energies = zeros(k)
            for c in 1:k
                # Data term
                energies[c] = (image[i,j] - centers[c])^2
                
                # Spatial term
                for (di, dj) in [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]
                    ni, nj = i + di, j + dj
                    if 1 ≤ ni ≤ n && 1 ≤ nj ≤ m
                        energies[c] -= β * (segmentation[ni, nj] == c ? 1 : 0)
                    end
                end
            end
            
            # Choose label with minimum energy
            new_label = argmin(energies)
            if new_label != segmentation[i, j]
                segmentation[i, j] = new_label
                changed = true
            end
        end
        
        if !changed
            break
        end
    end
    
    return segmentation, centers
end

# Generate synthetic noisy image
function create_test_image(n::Int)
    image = zeros(n, n)
    image[1:n÷2, 1:n÷2] .= 1.0
    image[1:n÷2, n÷2+1:end] .= 0.5
    image[n÷2+1:end, 1:n÷2] .= 0.5
    image[n÷2+1:end, n÷2+1:end] .= 0.0
    
    # Add noise
    image .+ 0.1 * randn(n, n)
end

# Test segmentation
test_image = create_test_image(50)
segmentation, centers = image_segmentation_potts(test_image, 4, 1.0)

# Visualize results
p1 = heatmap(test_image, title="Original Noisy Image")
p2 = heatmap(segmentation, title="Segmentation Result")
plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2))

6.7 8.7 Connections to Other Areas

Markov random fields connect spatial stochastic processes to numerous other areas within the curriculum. The Gibbs sampling techniques developed here extend the Markov chain Monte Carlo methods from Chapters 1-2 to spatial settings. The connection to Gaussian processes through the SPDE approach bridges this chapter with function space methods. In machine learning applications, MRFs form the foundation for conditional random fields and graphical models. The precision matrix structures studied here also connect to sparse matrix computations and numerical linear algebra, while the statistical applications in disease mapping and image analysis demonstrate the practical importance of spatial modeling across multiple domains.

6.8 8.8 Exercises

  1. Computational: Ising Model Phase Transition Implement a function to compute the magnetization m = (1/n²)∑ᵢⱼ xᵢⱼ for the 2D Ising model. Study how the magnetization varies with temperature T = 1/β for β ∈ [0.2, 0.8] on a 20×20 grid. Plot the magnetization curve and identify the approximate critical temperature.

  2. Theoretical: Conditional Independence in GMRFs Prove that in a GMRF, X_i ⊥ X_j | X_{-(i,j)} if and only if Q_{ij} = 0, where Q is the precision matrix. Extend this result to show that X_A ⊥
